sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: Global equity markets experienced a sharp broad-based contraction in March, as the MSCI World Index declined 6.8%. Performance divergence was stark between Developed Markets (-6.0%) and Emerging Markets (-11.9%), with the latter bearing the brunt of geopolitical volatility. Major benchmarks mirrored this bearish sentiment: the S&P 500 fell 5.1%, the FTSE 100 retreated 5.6%, and the STOXX 600 dropped 6.5%. The most acute selling pressure was concentrated in the Asia-Pacific region, where the KOSPI and TAIEX plummeted 12.8% and 9.6% respectively, reflecting heightened sensitivity to regional instability and energy supply disruptions.

sections.title: Your Must-Know Market Themes
sections.content: Four key themes that shaped markets this month:
themes: Geopolitics & Energy Crisis: The escalation of kinetic conflict involving Iran in March 2026 fundamentally re-indexed global energy risk premiums. The resulting threats to maritime chokepoints triggered a parabolic move in energy benchmarks, with WTI surging 51.6% and EU TTF gas climbing 59%. This 'black swan' event has forced an immediate pivot in corporate strategy, as energy security now supersedes disinflationary expectations, placing heavy-industry margins under existential pressure across the Eurozone and North Asia.

Stagflationary Policy Path: The resurgence of stagflationary narratives dominated central bank discourse this month. Policymakers are navigating an acute 'policy trilemma'—balancing surging supply-side inflation against deteriorating growth signals. While the Fed has signaled a preference for 'looking through' energy shocks to protect the labor market, the UK and Europe face more aggressive pass-through effects. This has led to a violent repricing of the front-end (UK 2Y +88bps), as markets anticipate that central banks may be forced to tighten into a nascent slowdown to anchor inflation expectations.

Systemic Risk-Off Sentiment: March was characterized by a classic 'dash for cash' and flight to the US Dollar, though traditional hedges failed to provide their usual utility. The 11.6% decline in Gold suggests a period of forced liquidation as institutional players met margin calls elsewhere in their portfolios. This 'sell-everything' regime reflects deep-seated anxiety regarding global liquidity depth and the potential for a systemic credit event should the Middle East conflict see further horizontal escalation.

Technology & Growth Momentum Unwind: The high-beta technology sector, the primary engine of 2025 performance, faced a severe valuation compression in March. Rising discount rates—evidenced by the 38bps move in the US 10Y—combined with geopolitical threats to semiconductor supply chains in the APAC region to trigger a massive rotation. Investors moved aggressively out of 'AI-growth' proxies and into defensive 'barbell' strategies, favoring SOE banks, utilities, and energy infrastructure over high-multiple tech names.

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content: US indices outperformed global peers on a relative basis, falling 5.1%, bolstered by the economy's relative energy independence. Despite the geopolitical noise, domestic macro data signaled a cooling labor market, with non-farm payrolls significantly missing expectations at -92k and unemployment ticking up to 4.4%. While CPI remained stable at 2.4%, a hot PPI print of 3.4% indicated mounting pipeline pressures from tariffs. The downward revision of Q4 GDP to 0.7% highlights an underlying loss of momentum, yet consumer confidence remained surprisingly resilient at 91.8, suggesting the domestic private sector is currently absorbing the shock better than international counterparts.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content: European markets were the epicenter of macro concerns as '2022 muscle memory' regarding energy dependency resurfaced. The STOXX 600's 6.5% decline reflects a rapid downward adjustment in growth forecasts to 1.1% for 2026. Eurozone inflation surged to 2.5%, driven by energy costs, while consumer confidence cratered to -16.3. The PMI data told a bifurcated story: while Manufacturing showed marginal resilience, the Services sector dragged the Composite index down to 50.5. In the UK, a stagnant growth backdrop and an 88bps surge in 2Y yields underscored the stagflationary threat, with input prices rising at their fastest monthly pace since 1992.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content: APAC was the worst-performing region globally (MXAP -13.4%), hit by a combination of high energy import dependency and a reversal of overextended 'momentum' positioning. South Korea (KOSPI -12.8%) and Japan (NKY -13.2%) saw sharp corrections as the 'risk-off' trade triggered a mass exodus from cyclicals and exporters. Conversely, Chinese equities (CSI 300 -5.5%) showed relative resilience, supported by strong early-year macro prints and a defensive rotation into 'New Energy' ecosystems and high-yield SOEs. Japan's decline was particularly notable given the previous rally, with even the banking sector failing to find support despite increasing conviction of a BOJ policy hike.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content: The US Dollar asserted its dominance as the ultimate safe haven, with the DXY gaining 2.4% and the trade-weighted index rising 2.7%. This 'dollar smile' effect decimated G10 carries, with the CHF (-3.8%), NZD (-4.2%), and SEK (-4.7%) seeing aggressive sell-offs. In the EM space, currencies fell 3.1% in aggregate. A critical threshold was breached in Japan as USD/JPY moved above 160, intensifying the rhetoric around direct Ministry of Finance intervention to stabilize the yen amidst the broader risk-off environment.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content: WTI crude (+51.6%) and EU TTF gas (+59%) reflected the immediate 'war premium' following Middle East escalations. Conversely, precious metals suffered from a liquidity-driven rout; Gold fell 11.6% and Silver plummeted 19.9%, as investors liquidated liquid assets to cover losses in equity and credit markets. Industrial metals were mixed, with Aluminum gaining 11.5% on supply-side fears while the broader complex remained tethered to slowing global growth expectations.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content: Global sovereign yields shifted higher in a bear-flattening move as markets priced in persistent energy-led inflation. The UK Gilt market saw the most extreme volatility, with the 2Y yield jumping 88bps. In the US, the 10Y yield rose 38bps to reflect the rising term premium. Federal Reserve Chair Powell attempted to anchor the long end by suggesting the committee would 'look through' supply-side shocks, prioritizing the labor market over energy-driven headline inflation. Despite the hawkish undertones of the March meeting, the US market continues to price in a modest 10bps of easing by year-end, diverging sharply from the more urgent tightening priced into European curves.
